serving.description: 1 slice of white bread with cheese
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of one slice of white bread topped with a slice of cheese, approximately 60g total.

nutrient_source.title: White bread with cheese
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core nutrient data (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore for 'White bread with cheese'. Extended micronutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central using closest analogous entries for white bread and processed cheese, as HPB does not report these fields.

gi_source.title: White bread GI reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 75 for white bread used as an analogous estimate for white bread with cheese. The protein and fat from cheese may marginally lower the composite GI, but the primary carbohydrate source (white bread) dictates a high GI classification. Glycemic load calculated as (75 x 18.63g carbohydrate per 60g serving) / 100 = 13.97, rounded to 13.95.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit white bread with cheese due to its very high sodium content of 771mg/100g, which substantially exceeds the 600mg/100g upper threshold and can acutely elevate blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (75) from refined white bread with low dietary fibre (1.49g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit this food due to its high GI of 75 and low fibre content, which promote rapid postprandial glucose elevation; substituting wholegrain bread would significantly improve the glycaemic profile.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (771mg/100g) and saturated fat contribution from cheese
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of very high sodium (771mg/100g) and saturated fat (approximately 3.2g/100g) from processed cheese elevates cardiovascular risk; individuals with CVD should choose low-sodium cheese on wholegrain bread and monitor portion size carefully.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White bread with cheese is a simple, widely consumed dish consisting of slices of soft white bread paired with slices of cheese, typically processed or cheddar-style. It is assembled without cooking and served as a quick snack, breakfast item, or light lunch. The texture is soft and yielding from the white bread, with the cheese adding a creamy, slightly salty, and savory character. Nutritionally, this combination is notable for its high protein content (15.46g/100g) and very high calcium (838mg/100g) derived from the cheese, making it a useful source of these nutrients. However, the refined white bread base contributes a high glycemic index of approximately 75, and the low dietary fibre (1.49g/100g) means blood glucose rises rapidly after consumption. The sodium content is very high at 771mg/100g, driven by both the white bread and the processed cheese, making this food unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease without careful portion control. Clinically, this food is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without modification. Substituting wholegrain bread and low-sodium cheese would substantially improve the nutritional profile.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient data (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced directly from HPB Singapore, an approved primary source, with exact match. Extended micronutrient values estimated from USDA FoodData Central using closest analogous entries and documented in notes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, an approved GI source.
notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) are from HPB Singapore (exact match). Extended micronutrient values (saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g) were estimated from USDA FoodData Central using closest analogous entries for white bread and processed cheese, as HPB does not report these fields for this record. All per_100g values are numeric; no -1 sentinel values are used as reasonable estimates were available from USDA. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on white bread from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 60g serving: (75 x 18.63) / 100 = 13.97, reported as 13.95. This food is classified as Western/global and does not require Singaporean multilingual naming despite being sourced from HPB Singapore.
